**Premillennialism**, in [Christian eschatology](/wiki/Christian_eschatology "Christian eschatology"), is the belief that [Jesus](/wiki/Jesus "Jesus") will physically return to the Earth (the [Second Coming](/wiki/Second_Coming "Second Coming")) before the [Millennium](/wiki/Millennialism#Christianity "Millennialism"), heralding a literal thousand-year [messianic age](/wiki/Messianic_Age "Messianic Age") of peace. Premillennialism is based upon a [literal](/wiki/Biblical_literalism "Biblical literalism") interpretation of [Revelation 20](/wiki/Revelation_20 "Revelation 20") ([Revelation 20:1–6](https://bible.oremus.org/?passage=Revelation%2020:1–6&version=nrsv)) in the [New Testament](/wiki/New_Testament "New Testament"), which describes Jesus's reign in a period of a thousand years.

Premillennialism is in contrast to [amillennialism](/wiki/Amillennialism "Amillennialism") and [postmillennialism](/wiki/Postmillennialism "Postmillennialism") beliefs. Amillennialism interprets [Revelation 20:1–6](https://bible.oremus.org/?passage=Revelation%2020:1–6&version=nrsv) as pertaining to the present time, and holds that Christ currently reigns in [Heaven](/wiki/Heaven_(Christianity) "Heaven (Christianity)") with the departed [saints](/wiki/Saints "Saints"). This interpretation views the symbolism of Revelation as referring to a spiritual conflict between Heaven and [Hell](/wiki/Christian_views_on_Hell "Christian views on Hell") rather than a physical conflict on Earth. Amillennialists do not view the thousand years mentioned in Revelation as a literal thousand years, but see the number "thousand" as symbolic and [numerological](/wiki/Numerology "Numerology") and see the kingdom of Christ as already present in the church beginning with the [Pentecost](/wiki/Pentecost "Pentecost") in the book of [Acts](/wiki/Acts_of_the_Apostles "Acts of the Apostles"). Denominations such as [Oriental Orthodoxy](/wiki/Oriental_Orthodoxy "Oriental Orthodoxy"), [Eastern Orthodoxy](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodoxy "Eastern Orthodoxy"), [Catholicism](/wiki/Catholicism "Catholicism"), [Anglicanism](/wiki/Anglicanism "Anglicanism"), [Calvinism](/wiki/Calvinism "Calvinism")[[1]](#cite_note-1) and [Lutheranism](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism") are generally amillennial. Postmillennialism views the millennial rule as a [Golden Age](/wiki/Golden_Age "Golden Age") in which [Christian ethics](/wiki/Christian_ethics "Christian ethics") prosper through preaching and redemptive work,[[2]](#cite_note-2) but occurring before the second coming.

Premillennialism is often used to refer specifically to those who adhere to the beliefs in an earthly millennial reign of Christ as well as a [rapture](/wiki/Rapture "Rapture") of the faithful coming before (dispensational) or after (historic) the [Great Tribulation](/wiki/Great_Tribulation "Great Tribulation") preceding the Millennium. In the 20th century, the belief became common in [Evangelicalism](/wiki/Evangelicalism "Evangelicalism") according to surveys on this topic.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

## Terminology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Premillennialism&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Terminology")]

The current religious term *premillennialism* did not come into use until the mid-19th century. The word's coinage was "almost entirely the work of [British](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") and [American](/wiki/United_States "United States") [Protestants](/wiki/Protestantism "Protestantism") and was prompted by their belief that the [French](/wiki/The_French_Revolution "The French Revolution") and [American Revolutions](/wiki/American_Revolution "American Revolution") (the French, especially) realized [prophecies](/wiki/Prophecy "Prophecy") made in the books of [Daniel](/wiki/Book_of_Daniel "Book of Daniel") and [Revelation](/wiki/Book_of_Revelation "Book of Revelation")."[[4]](#cite_note-4)

### Other views

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Premillennialism&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Other views")]

The proponents of [amillennialism](/wiki/Amillennialism "Amillennialism") interpret the millennium as being a symbolic period of time, which is consistent with the highly symbolic nature of the literary and apocalyptic genre of the Book of Revelation, sometimes indicating that the thousand years represent God's rule over his creation or the [Church](/wiki/Christian_Church "Christian Church").[[5]](#cite_note-5)

[Postmillennialists](/wiki/Postmillennialism "Postmillennialism") hold to the view that the Second Coming will happen after the millennium.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Premillennialism&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: History")]

### Justin Martyr and Irenaeus

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Premillennialism&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Justin Martyr and Irenaeus")]

[Justin Martyr](/wiki/Justin_Martyr "Justin Martyr") in the 2nd century was one of the first Christian writers to clearly describe himself as continuing in the "Jewish" belief of a temporary messianic kingdom prior to the eternal state, although the notion of Millennium in his *[Dialogue with Trypho](/wiki/Dialogue_with_Trypho "Dialogue with Trypho")* seem to differ from that of the Apology.[[7]](#cite_note-7) According to Johannes Quasten, "In his eschatological ideas Justin shares the views of the Chiliasts concerning the millennium."[[8]](#cite_note-8) He maintains a premillennial distinction, namely that there would be two resurrections, one of believers before [Jesus's](/wiki/Jesus "Jesus") reign and then a general resurrection afterwards. Justin wrote in chapter 80 of his work *Dialogue with Trypho*, "I and others who are right-minded Christians on all points are assured that there will be a resurrection of the dead, and a thousand years in Jerusalem, which will then be built... For Isaiah spoke in that manner concerning this period of a thousand years." Though he conceded earlier in the same chapter that his view was not universal by saying that he "and many who belong to the pure and pious faith, and are true Christians, think otherwise."[[9]](#cite_note-9)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/1/13/Saint_Irenaeus.jpg)](/wiki/File:Saint_Irenaeus.jpg)

St. [Irenaeus](/wiki/Irenaeus "Irenaeus") (*c.* 130–202), an early Christian Premillennialist

[Irenaeus](/wiki/Irenaeus "Irenaeus"), the late 2nd century [bishop of Lyon](/wiki/Bishop_of_Lyon "Bishop of Lyon"), was an outspoken premillennialist. He is best known for his voluminous tome written against the 2nd century Gnostic threat, commonly called *[Against Heresies](/wiki/On_the_Detection_and_Overthrow_of_the_So-Called_Gnosis "On the Detection and Overthrow of the So-Called Gnosis")*. In the fifth book of *Against Heresies*, Irenaeus concentrates primarily on eschatology. In one passage he defends premillennialism by arguing that a future earthly kingdom is necessary because of [God's promise to Abraham](/wiki/Abrahamic_covenant "Abrahamic covenant"), he wrote "The promise remains steadfast... God promised him the inheritance of the land. Yet, Abraham did not receive it during all the time of his journey there. Accordingly, it must be that Abraham, together with his seed (that is, those who fear God and believe in Him), will receive it at the resurrection of the just."[[10]](#cite_note-10) In another place Irenaeus also explained that the blessing to [Jacob](/wiki/Jacob "Jacob") "belongs unquestionably to the times of the kingdom when the righteous will bear rule, after their rising from the dead. It is also the time when the creation will bear fruit with an abundance of all kinds of food, having been renovated and set free... And all of the animals will feed on the vegetation of the earth... and they will be in perfect submission to man. And these things are borne witness to in the fourth book of the writings of [Papias](/wiki/Papias_of_Hierapolis "Papias of Hierapolis"), the hearer of [John](/wiki/John_of_Patmos "John of Patmos"), and a companion of [Polycarp](/wiki/Polycarp "Polycarp")." (5.33.3) Apparently Irenaeus also held to the sexta-/septamillennial scheme writing that the end of human history will occur after the 6,000th year. (5.28.3).[[11]](#cite_note-11)

### Other ante-Nicene premillennialists

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Premillennialism&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Other ante-Nicene premillennialists")]

Irenaeus and Justin represent two of the most outspoken premillennialists of the pre-Nicean church. Other early premillennialists included [Pseudo-Barnabas](/wiki/Epistle_of_Barnabas "Epistle of Barnabas"),[[12]](#cite_note-12) [Papias](/wiki/Papias_of_Hierapolis "Papias of Hierapolis"),[[13]](#cite_note-13) [Methodius](/wiki/Methodius_of_Olympus "Methodius of Olympus"), [Lactantius](/wiki/Lactantius "Lactantius"),[[14]](#cite_note-14) [Commodianus](/wiki/Commodianus "Commodianus")[[15]](#cite_note-15) [Theophilus](/wiki/Theophilus_of_Antioch "Theophilus of Antioch"), [Tertullian](/wiki/Tertullian "Tertullian"),[[16]](#cite_note-16) [Melito](/wiki/Melito_of_Sardis "Melito of Sardis"),[[17]](#cite_note-17) [Hippolytus of Rome](/wiki/Hippolytus_of_Rome "Hippolytus of Rome"), [Victorinus of Pettau](/wiki/Victorinus_of_Pettau "Victorinus of Pettau")[[18]](#cite_note-18)[[19]](#cite_note-19) and various Gnostics groups and the [Montanists](/wiki/Montanists "Montanists"). Many of these theologians and others in the early church expressed their belief in premillennialism through their acceptance of the sexta-septamillennial tradition. This belief claims that human history will continue for 6,000 years and then will enjoy Sabbath for 1,000 years (the millennial kingdom), thus all of human history will have a total of 7,000 years prior to the [new creation](/wiki/World_to_Come "World to Come").

### Ante-Nicene opposition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Premillennialism&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Ante-Nicene opposition")]

The first clear opponent of premillennialism associated with Christianity was the gnostic [Marcion](/wiki/Marcion "Marcion"). Marcion opposed the use of the Old Testament and most books of the New Testament that were not written by the [apostle Paul](/wiki/Apostle_Paul "Apostle Paul"). Regarding Marcion and premillennialism, Harvard scholar H. Brown noted,

:   The first great heretic broke drastically with the faith of the early church in abandoning the doctrine of the imminent, personal return of Christ...Marcion did not believe in a real [incarnation](/wiki/Incarnation "Incarnation"), and consequently there was no logical place in his system for a real Second Coming...Marcion expected the majority of mankind to be lost...he denied the validity of the Old Testament and its Law...As the first great heretic, Marcion developed and perfected his heterodox system before orthodoxy had fully defined itself...Marcion represents a movement that so radically transformed the Christian doctrine of God and Christ that it can hardly be said to be Christian.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

Throughout the [Patristic](/wiki/Patristics "Patristics") period—particularly in the 3rd century—there had been rising opposition to premillennialism. [Origen](/wiki/Origen "Origen") was the first to challenge the doctrine openly. Through [allegorical](/wiki/Allegory "Allegory") interpretation, he had been a proponent of amillennialism (of course, the sexta-septamillennial tradition was itself based upon similar means of allegorical interpretation).[[21]](#cite_note-21) Although Origen was not always wholly "orthodox" in his theology, he had at one point completely spiritualized Christ's second coming prophesied in the New Testament. Origen did this in his *Commentary on Matthew*[[22]](#cite_note-22) when he taught that "Christ's return signifies His disclosure of Himself and His deity to all humanity in such a way that all might partake of His glory to the degree that each individual's actions warrant (*Commentary on Matthew* 12.30)."[[23]](#cite_note-23) Even Origen's milder forms of this teaching left no room for a literal millennium and it was so extreme that few actually followed it. But his influence did gain wider acceptance especially in the period following [Constantine](/wiki/Constantine_I "Constantine I").

Dionysius of Alexandria stood against premillennialism when the [chiliastic](/wiki/Millennialism "Millennialism") work, [*The Refutation of the Allegorizers*](/wiki/Book_of_Nepos "Book of Nepos") written by Nepos, a [bishop](/wiki/Bishop "Bishop") in [Egypt](/wiki/Egypt "Egypt") became popular in [Alexandria](/wiki/Alexandria "Alexandria"). Dionysius argued against Nepos's influence and convinced the churches of the region of amillennialism. The church historian, [Eusebius](/wiki/Eusebius "Eusebius"), reports this in his *[Ecclesiastical History](/wiki/Church_History_(Eusebius) "Church History (Eusebius)")*.[[24]](#cite_note-24) Eusebius also had low regard for the chiliast, Papias, and he let it be known that in his opinion Papias was "a man of small mental capacity" because he had taken the Apocalypse literally.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

### Middle Ages and the Reformation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Premillennialism&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Middle Ages and the Reformation")]

#### Augustinian eschatological foundation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Premillennialism&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Augustinian eschatological foundation")]

[Oxford theologian](/wiki/University_of_Oxford "University of Oxford") [Alister McGrath](/wiki/Alister_McGrath "Alister McGrath") has noted that "all [medieval](/wiki/Medieval "Medieval") theology is ['Augustinian'](/wiki/Augustine_of_Hippo "Augustine of Hippo") to a greater or lesser extent."[[26]](#cite_note-26) Augustine's (354–430) influence shaped not only the [Western](/wiki/Western_Christianity "Western Christianity") [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages"), but it also influenced the [Protestant reformers](/wiki/Protestant_Reformation "Protestant Reformation"), who constantly referred to his teaching in their own debates. His teaching is "still one of the most potent elements in Western religious thought."[[27]](#cite_note-27) Therefore, to analyze what happened to premillennialism in the Middle Ages and the Reformation, it is necessary to observe the Augustinian foundation.

In his early period, Augustine held to the sexta-/septamillennial view common in early Christianity (see above section on Patristic Age).[[28]](#cite_note-28) In accordance with this view, Augustine divided history into two separate dispensations, first the church age (the current age of 6,000 years), and then the millennial kingdom (*Sermon 259.2*). Nevertheless, early in his career Augustine converted from premillennialism to amillennialism. Anderson locates three reasons that may account for Augustine's theological shift:

1. A reaction to [Donatist](/wiki/Donatist "Donatist") excess – Augustine displayed a revulsion to the Donatists' bacchanal feasts which seemingly used excessive amounts of food and drink (*City of God*, 20.7).[[29]](#cite_note-29) The Donatists were premillennial and thus Augustine formed a connection between their sensual behavior and their earthly eschatological expectation.
2. A reaction to eschatological [sensationalism](/wiki/Sensationalism "Sensationalism") – The millennial fervor of premillennialists as the year AD 500 was nearing caused them to have overly jovial celebrations (some septa-/sextamillennial interpreters calculated Jesus's birth to have happened 5,500 years after creation).[[30]](#cite_note-30) These feasts appeared to Augustine to take more pleasure in the physical world than the spiritual. Such earthly revelry was repulsive to Augustine since he placed little value on the material world.[[31]](#cite_note-31)
3. A preference for allegorical interpretation – Finally, Augustine was influenced by the popular allegorical interpretation of Scripture, particularly of The Book of Revelation. [Tyconius](/wiki/Tyconius "Tyconius") (d. *c*. 400), a Donatist [lay](/wiki/Laity "Laity") theologian, "whose reinterpretation of his culture's [separatist](/wiki/Separatism "Separatism") and millenarian traditions provided the point of departure for what is more brilliant and idiosyncratic in Augustine's own theology. And it is Tyconius, most precisely, whose own reading of John's Apocalypse determined the Western church's exegesis for the next eight hundred years."[[32]](#cite_note-32)

After moving away from premillennialism to amillennialism, Augustine viewed Sabbath rest in the sexta-/septamillennial scheme as "symbolically representative of Eternity." Moreover, the millennium of Revelation 20 became for him "symbolically representative of Christ’s present reign with the saints."[[33]](#cite_note-33) [Richard Landes](/wiki/Richard_Landes "Richard Landes") observed the 4th century as a time of major shift for Christian eschatology by noting that it "marked a crucial moment in the history of millenarianism, since during this period Augustine repudiated even the allegorizing variety he himself had previously accepted. From this point on he dedicated much of his energy to ridding the church of this [belief](/wiki/Doctrine "Doctrine")."[[34]](#cite_note-34)

#### Medieval and Reformation amillennialism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Premillennialism&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Medieval and Reformation amillennialism")]

Augustine's later amillennial view laid the eschatological foundation for the Middle Ages, which practically abandoned premillennialism.[[35]](#cite_note-35) The theological term "kingdom" maintained its eschatological function, though it was not necessarily futuristic. Instead it consistently referred to the present age so that the church was currently experiencing the eschaton. [Julian of Toledo](/wiki/Julian_of_Toledo "Julian of Toledo") (642–690) summarizes the medieval doctrine of the millennium by referring to it as "the church of God which, by the diffusion of its faith and works, is spread out as a kingdom of faith from the time of the incarnation until the time of the coming judgment".[[36]](#cite_note-36)

A notable exception to normative medieval eschatology is found in [Joachim of Fiore](/wiki/Joachim_of_Fiore "Joachim of Fiore") (c. 1135–1202), a [Cistercian](/wiki/Cistercian "Cistercian") monk, who to an extent, stressed premillennial themes. Joachim divided earth's history into three periods. He assigned each age to a particular person of the [Trinity](/wiki/Trinity "Trinity") as the guiding principle of that era. The first era was the Old Testament history and was accordingly the age of the [Father](/wiki/God_the_Father "God the Father"); the current age of the church was the age of the [Son](/wiki/God_the_Son "God the Son"); and still in Joachim's future was the age of the [Spirit](/wiki/God_the_Holy_Spirit "God the Holy Spirit"). For Joachim, 1260 was to mark the end of the second and the beginning of the third and final golden age of earth's history.[[37]](#cite_note-37)

During the Reformation period, amillennialism continued to be the popular view of the Reformers. The [Lutherans](/wiki/Lutherans "Lutherans") formally rejected chiliasm (millennialism) in [The Augsburg Confession](/wiki/The_Augsburg_Confession "The Augsburg Confession"). "Art. XVII., condemns the [Anabaptists](/wiki/Anabaptists "Anabaptists") and others 'who now scatter Jewish opinions that, before the resurrection of the dead, the godly shall occupy the kingdom of the world, the wicked being everywhere suppressed.'"[[38]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-38) Likewise, the [Swiss Reformer](/wiki/Reformation_in_Switzerland "Reformation in Switzerland") [Heinrich Bullinger](/wiki/Heinrich_Bullinger "Heinrich Bullinger") wrote up the [Second Helvetic Confession](/wiki/Helvetic_Confessions "Helvetic Confessions"), which reads "We also reject the Jewish dream of a millennium, or golden age on earth, before the last judgment."[[39]](#cite_note-39) Furthermore, [John Calvin](/wiki/John_Calvin "John Calvin") wrote in *Institutes* that millennialism is a "fiction" that is "too childish either to need or to be worth a refutation".[[40]](#cite_note-40) The [Anglican Church](/wiki/Anglican_Church "Anglican Church") originally formalized a statement against millenarianism in the [Anglican Articles](/wiki/Thirty-Nine_Articles "Thirty-Nine Articles"). This is observed in the 41st of the Anglican Articles, drawn up by [Thomas Cranmer](/wiki/Thomas_Cranmer "Thomas Cranmer") (1553), describing the millennium as a 'fable of Jewish dotage', but it was omitted at a later time in the revision under [Elizabeth](/wiki/Elizabeth_I_of_England "Elizabeth I of England") (1563).[[38]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-38)

Contrarily, certain Anabaptists, [Huguenots](/wiki/Huguenots "Huguenots"), and [Bohemian Brethren](/wiki/Bohemian_Brethren "Bohemian Brethren") were premillennial. [Michael Servetus](/wiki/Michael_Servetus "Michael Servetus") taught a chiliastic view, though he was denounced by the Reformers as a heretic and executed in [Geneva](/wiki/Geneva "Geneva") under Calvin's authority.[[41]](#cite_note-41) A few in the mainstream accepted it, such as [Joseph Mede](/wiki/Joseph_Mede "Joseph Mede") (1586–1638)[[42]](#cite_note-42) and possibly [Hugh Latimer](/wiki/Hugh_Latimer "Hugh Latimer") (died 1555),[[43]](#cite_note-43) but it was never a conventional belief throughout the period.

### Modern era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Premillennialism&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Modern era")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/89/Millennial_views.svg/500px-Millennial_views.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Millennial_views.svg)

Comparison of Christian millennial interpretations

#### 17th and 18th centuries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Premillennialism&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: 17th and 18th centuries")]

Premillennialism experienced a revival among 17th century Puritans like Thomas Brightman, Joseph Mede, and others.[[44]](#cite_note-44) Although they were not premillennial, the [English](/wiki/England "England") [theologian](/wiki/Theologian "Theologian") [Daniel Whitby](/wiki/Daniel_Whitby "Daniel Whitby") (1688–1726), the German [Johann Albrecht Bengel](/wiki/Johann_Albrecht_Bengel "Johann Albrecht Bengel") (1687–1752), and the American [Jonathan Edwards](/wiki/Jonathan_Edwards_(theologian) "Jonathan Edwards (theologian)") (1703–58) "fueled millennial ideas with new influence in the nineteenth century."[[45]](#cite_note-45) It was authors such as these who concluded that the decline of the [Roman Catholic Church](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Church "Roman Catholic Church") would make way for the conversion and restoration of the nation of [Israel](/wiki/Israel "Israel"). Edwards taught that a type of Millennium would occur "1260 years after A.D. 606 when Rome was recognized as having universal authority."[[46]](#cite_note-46) His [Puritan](/wiki/Puritan "Puritan") contemporaries, [Increase Mather](/wiki/Increase_Mather "Increase Mather") and [Cotton Mather](/wiki/Cotton_Mather "Cotton Mather"), openly proclaimed a belief in a literal millennium. Increase Mather wrote "That which presseth me so, as that I cannot gainsay the Chiliastical opinion, is that I take these things for Principles, and no way doubt but that they are demonstrable. 1. That the thousand apocalyptical years are not passed but future. 2. That the coming of Christ to raise the dead and to judge the earth will be within much less than this thousand years. 3. That the conversion of the [Jews](/wiki/Jew "Jew") will not be till this present state of the world is near unto its end. 4. That, after the Jews' conversion there will be a glorious day for the elect upon earth, and that this day shall be a very long continuance."[[47]](#cite_note-47)

#### 19th century to present

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Premillennialism&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: 19th century to present")]

Between 1790 and the mid-19th century, premillennialism was a popular view among English [Evangelicals](/wiki/Evangelicals "Evangelicals"), even within the Anglican church. [Thomas Macaulay](/wiki/Thomas_Babington_Macaulay,_1st_Baron_Macaulay "Thomas Babington Macaulay, 1st Baron Macaulay") observed this and wrote "Many Christians believe that the Messiah will shortly establish a kingdom on the earth, and visibly reign over all its inhabitants."[[48]](#cite_note-48)
Throughout the 19th century, premillennialism continued to gain wider acceptance in both the US and in Britain, particularly among the [Irvingites](/wiki/Irvingites "Irvingites"),[[49]](#cite_note-49) [Plymouth Brethren](/wiki/Plymouth_Brethren "Plymouth Brethren"), [Christadelphians](/wiki/Christadelphians "Christadelphians"),[[50]](#cite_note-50) [Church of God](/wiki/Church_of_God_(Cleveland,_Tennessee) "Church of God (Cleveland, Tennessee)"), [Christian Israelite Church](/wiki/Christian_Israelite_Church "Christian Israelite Church").[[51]](#cite_note-51) Premillennialism continues to be popular among Evangelical, [Fundamentalist Christian](/wiki/Fundamentalist_Christianity "Fundamentalist Christianity"), and [Living Church of God](/wiki/Living_Church_of_God "Living Church of God") communities in the 20th and 21st centuries,[[52]](#cite_note-whalen-52) expanding further into the churches of [Asia](/wiki/Asia "Asia"), [Africa](/wiki/Africa "Africa") and [South America](/wiki/South_America "South America").

Many traditional denominations continue to oppose the concept of a literal millennial kingdom.[[52]](#cite_note-whalen-52) The [Lutheran Church–Missouri Synod](/wiki/Lutheran_Church%E2%80%93Missouri_Synod "Lutheran Church–Missouri Synod") explicitly states, "When Christ returns, 'new heavens and a new earth' will be created ([2 Pet.](/wiki/2_Peter "2 Peter") 3:10-13)." The [catechism of the Catholic Church](/wiki/Catechism_of_the_Catholic_Church "Catechism of the Catholic Church") teaches in paragraph 676 that the millennium is to be understood as "beyond history":

> The Antichrist's deception already begins to take shape in the world every time the claim is made to realize within history that messianic hope which can only be realized beyond history through the eschatological judgment. The Church has rejected even modified forms of this falsification of the kingdom to come under the name of millenarianism, especially the "intrinsically perverse" political form of a secular messianism.

— Catechism of the Catholic Church, para. 676

Whalen has noted that modern premillennialism is "criticized roundly for naïve scholarship which confuses the [poetic](/wiki/Poetry "Poetry") and inspirational [prose](/wiki/Prose "Prose") of prophecy with [fortune telling](/wiki/Fortune_telling "Fortune telling")", though "Premillennialists retort that they merely follow the Word of God, regardless of ridicule." He then notes that, nevertheless, "the virtual theology which surrounds premillennialism is today stronger and more widely spread than at any time in history."[[53]](#cite_note-53)

## Historic vs. dispensational schools

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Premillennialism&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Historic vs. dispensational schools")]

Contemporary premillennialism is divided into two schools of thought.

### Historic school

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Premillennialism&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Historic school")]

Main article: [Historic premillennialism](/wiki/Historic_premillennialism "Historic premillennialism")

Historic, or classic, premillennialism is distinctively non-dispensational. This means that it sees no radical theological distinction between [Israel](/wiki/Israel "Israel") and the Church. It is often [posttribulational](/wiki/Post-tribulation_rapture "Post-tribulation rapture"), meaning that the rapture of the church will occur after a period of [tribulation](/wiki/Post_Tribulation_Rapture "Post Tribulation Rapture"). Historic premillennialism maintains chiliasm because of its view that the church will be caught up to meet Christ in the air and then escort him to the earth in order to share in his literal thousand year rule. Proponents of the view include John Gill, Mike Placko, [Charles Spurgeon](/wiki/Charles_Spurgeon "Charles Spurgeon"),[[54]](#cite_note-54) [James Montgomery Boice](/wiki/James_Montgomery_Boice "James Montgomery Boice"),[[55]](#cite_note-:0-55) [George Eldon Ladd](/wiki/George_Eldon_Ladd "George Eldon Ladd"),[[55]](#cite_note-:0-55) [John Piper](/wiki/John_Piper_(theologian) "John Piper (theologian)"),[[56]](#cite_note-56) [Albert Mohler](/wiki/Albert_Mohler "Albert Mohler"),[[57]](#cite_note-57) [Francis Schaeffer](/wiki/Francis_Schaeffer "Francis Schaeffer"), [Carl F. H. Henry](/wiki/Carl_F._H._Henry "Carl F. H. Henry"),[[58]](#cite_note-58) [Harold Lindsell](/wiki/Harold_Lindsell "Harold Lindsell"), [D. A. Carson](/wiki/D._A._Carson "D. A. Carson"),[[59]](#cite_note-59)[[60]](#cite_note-60) [Bryan Chapell](/wiki/Bryan_Chapell "Bryan Chapell"),[[61]](#cite_note-61) and [Gordon Clark](/wiki/Gordon_Clark "Gordon Clark").[[62]](#cite_note-62)

### Dispensational school

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Premillennialism&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Dispensational school")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/5/59/CISCOFIELD.JPG)](/wiki/File:CISCOFIELD.JPG)

C.I. Scofield popularized dispensational premillennialism through the Scofield Reference Bible.

Main article: [Dispensationalism](/wiki/Dispensationalism "Dispensationalism")

Dispensational premillennialism[[63]](#cite_note-63) generally holds that Israel and the Church are distinct entities.[[64]](#cite_note-64) It also widely holds to the pretribulational return of [Christ](/wiki/Christ "Christ"), which believes that Jesus will return to take up Christians into heaven by means of a rapture immediately before a seven-year worldwide tribulation. This will be followed by an additional return of Christ with his saints (though there are [posttribulation](/wiki/Posttribulation_rapture "Posttribulation rapture") dispensationalists, such as [Robert Gundry](/wiki/Robert_Gundry "Robert Gundry")).

Dispensationalism traces its roots to the 1830s and [John Nelson Darby](/wiki/John_Nelson_Darby "John Nelson Darby") (1800–1882), an Anglican churchman and an early leader of the Plymouth Brethren. In the US, the dispensational form of premillennialism was propagated on the popular level largely through the [Scofield Reference Bible](/wiki/Scofield_Reference_Bible "Scofield Reference Bible") and on the academic level with [Lewis Sperry Chafer](/wiki/Lewis_Sperry_Chafer "Lewis Sperry Chafer")'s eight-volume *Systematic Theology*. More recently dispensational eschatology has been popularized through [Hal Lindsey](/wiki/Hal_Lindsey "Hal Lindsey")'s 1970s bestseller, *[The Late, Great Planet Earth](/wiki/The_Late,_Great_Planet_Earth "The Late, Great Planet Earth")* and through the *[Left Behind](/wiki/Left_Behind "Left Behind")* Series by [Tim Lahaye](/wiki/Tim_Lahaye "Tim Lahaye") and [Jerry Jenkins](/wiki/Jerry_Jenkins "Jerry Jenkins"). Popular proponents of dispensational premillennialism have been [John F. MacArthur](/wiki/John_F._MacArthur "John F. MacArthur"), Phil Johnson, [Ray Comfort](/wiki/Ray_Comfort "Ray Comfort"), [Jerry Falwell](/wiki/Jerry_Falwell "Jerry Falwell"), [Todd Friel](/w/index.php?title=Todd_Friel&action=edit&redlink=1 "Todd Friel (page does not exist)"), [Dwight Pentecost](/wiki/J._Dwight_Pentecost "J. Dwight Pentecost"), [John Walvoord](/wiki/John_Walvoord "John Walvoord"), [Tim Lahaye](/wiki/Tim_Lahaye "Tim Lahaye"), [Charles Caldwell Ryrie](/wiki/Charles_Caldwell_Ryrie "Charles Caldwell Ryrie"), [Norman Geisler](/wiki/Norman_Geisler "Norman Geisler"), [Erwin Lutzer](/wiki/Erwin_Lutzer "Erwin Lutzer"), and [Charles L. Feinberg](/wiki/Charles_L._Feinberg "Charles L. Feinberg"). [Craig Blaising](/wiki/Craig_Blaising "Craig Blaising") and [Darrell Bock](/wiki/Darrell_Bock "Darrell Bock") have developed a form of dispensationalism that is growing in popularity known as [progressive dispensationalism](/wiki/Progressive_dispensationalism "Progressive dispensationalism"). This view understands that an aspect of the eschatological kingdom presently exists, but must wait for the millennium to be realized fully.[[65]](#cite_note-65)

Simonetti, “Millenarism” in *Encyclopedia of the Early Church*, Translated by Adrian Walford, Volume 1 (New York: [Oxford University Press](/wiki/Oxford_University_Press "Oxford University Press"), 1992), 560. It is doubtless that Origen respected apostolic tradition in interpretation. It was Origen himself who said "Non debemus credere nisi quemadmodum per successionem Ecclesiae Dei tradiderunt nobis" (In Matt., ser. 46, [Migne](/wiki/Migne "Migne"), XIII, 1667). However as it is noted in [The Catholic Encyclopedia](http://www.newadvent.org/cathen/11306b.htm) "Origen has recourse too easily to allegorism to explain purely apparent antilogies or antinomies. He considers that certain narratives or ordinances of the Bible would be unworthy of God if they had to be taken according to the letter, or if they were to be taken solely according to the letter. He justifies the allegorism by the fact that otherwise certain accounts or certain precepts now abrogated would be useless and profitless for the reader: a fact which appears to him contrary to the providence of the Divine inspirer and the dignity of Holy Writ."

Review and Herald, 1946–54. ASIN B0006AR2YQ. An enormously comprehensive history of eschatological thought. Froom is an [Adventist](/wiki/Seventh-day_Adventist_Church "Seventh-day Adventist Church") but this is not overly apparent in the work. It is currently out of print.
* Hill, Charles F. *Regnum Caelorum: Patterns of Millennial Thought in Early Christianity*. Grand Rapids: Eerdmans, 2001. [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [0-8028-4634-3](/wiki/Special:BookSources/0-8028-4634-3 "Special:BookSources/0-8028-4634-3"). Hill questions the legitimacy of early premillennial thought by analyzing an apparent paradox in the early chiliast theology, particularly the [intermediate state](/wiki/Hades "Hades").
* [Marsden, George](/wiki/George_Marsden "George Marsden"). *Fundamentalism and American Culture*, Oxford: Oxford University Press, 2022 (3rd Ed.) [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [978-0197599495](/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-0197599495 "Special:BookSources/978-0197599495"). The history of late 19th C.-present premillenialism in the United States and its impact on the general culture.
* Mühling, Markus, "Grundwissen Eschatologie. Systematische Theologie aus der Perspektive der Hoffnung", Göttingen: [Vandenhoeck & Ruprecht](/wiki/Vandenhoeck_%26_Ruprecht "Vandenhoeck & Ruprecht"), 2005. [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [978-3-8252-2918-4](/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-3-8252-2918-4 "Special:BookSources/978-3-8252-2918-4"), 209–214.
